title: Firewall Rule Hit Rate Trending
app: `Splunk_TA_paloalto`, `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, Cisco Secure Firewall Add-on, `Splunk_TA_juniper` (SRX), Splunk Common Information Model
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count FROM datamodel=Network_Traffic WHERE nodename=All_Traffic All_Traffic.action IN ("allowed","accept") earliest=-30d
    BY _time span=1d All_Traffic.rule
| `drop_dm_object_name(All_Traffic)`
| timechart span=1d sum(count) as hits by rule useother=f limit=10
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as agg_value from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port span=1d | sort - agg_value
description: Top rules by hit count over 30 days expose noisy policies, overly broad permits, and candidates for segmentation review. Flat or rising hit rates on â€ścatch-allâ€ť rules often precede policy hygiene projects.
value: Top rules by hit count over 30 days expose noisy policies, overly broad permits, and candidates for segmentation review. Flat or rising hit rates on â€ścatch-allâ€ť rules often precede policy hygiene projects.
grandmaExplanation: Top rules by hit count over 30 days expose noisy policies, overly broad permits, and candidates for segmentation review â€” so we know when protection is off, out of date, or bypassed.
implementation: If `rule` is missing, derive from `policy_id` or `rule_uid` via vendor TA. Exclude health-check and internal monitoring sources with a subnet lookup. Use accelerated DM or summary `sourcetype=stash` for very high volume. Pair with change tickets when hit rates jump after policy pushes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
â€˘ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Splunk_TA_paloalto`, `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, Cisco Secure Firewall Add-on, `Splunk_TA_juniper` (SRX), Splunk Common Information Model.
â€˘ Ensure the following data sources are available: `Network_Traffic` data model (firewall allow/deny), `index=network` `sourcetype=pan:traffic` or equivalent.
â€˘ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 â€” Configure data collection
If `rule` is missing, derive from `policy_id` or `rule_uid` via vendor TA. Exclude health-check and internal monitoring sources with a subnet lookup. Use accelerated DM or summary `sourcetype=stash` for very high volume. Pair with change tickets when hit rates jump after policy pushes.

Step 2 â€” Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count FROM datamodel=Network_Traffic WHERE nodename=All_Traffic All_Traffic.action IN ("allowed","accept") earliest=-30d
    BY _time span=1d All_Traffic.rule
| `drop_dm_object_name(All_Traffic)`
| timechart span=1d sum(count) as hits by rule useother=f limit=10
```

Understanding this SPL

**Firewall Rule Hit Rate Trending** â€” Top rules by hit count over 30 days expose noisy policies, overly broad permits, and candidates for segmentation review. Flat or rising hit rates on â€ścatch-allâ€ť rules often precede policy hygiene projects.

Documented **Data sources**: `Network_Traffic` data model (firewall allow/deny), `index=network` `sourcetype=pan:traffic` or equivalent. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_paloalto`, `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, Cisco Secure Firewall Add-on, `Splunk_TA_juniper` (SRX), Splunk Common Information Model. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic` â€” enable acceleration for that model.
â€˘ Invokes macro `drop_dm_object_name(All_Traffic)` â€” in Search, use the UI or expand to inspect the underlying SPL.
â€˘ `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1d** buckets with a separate series **by rule useother=f limit=10** â€” ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t sum(All_Traffic.bytes_in) as agg_value from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port span=1d | sort - agg_value
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Firewall Rule Hit Rate Trending** â€” Top rules by hit count over 30 days expose noisy policies, overly broad permits, and candidates for segmentation review. Flat or rising hit rates on â€ścatch-allâ€ť rules often precede policy hygiene projects.

Documented **Data sources**: `Network_Traffic` data model (firewall allow/deny), `index=network` `sourcetype=pan:traffic` or equivalent. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Splunk_TA_paloalto`, `TA-fortinet_fortigate`, Cisco Secure Firewall Add-on, `Splunk_TA_juniper` (SRX), Splunk Common Information Model. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feedâ€”rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

â€˘ Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` â€” enable acceleration for that model.
â€˘ Orders rows with `sort` â€” combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 â€” Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 â€” Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (top rules by hits), line chart (daily hits for top five rules), treemap (rule share of total hits).
visualization: Bar chart (top rules by hits), line chart (daily hits for top five rules), treemap (rule share of total hits).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern â€” correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
